meta.description: Research notes for future MrAI experiments

sections.title: Canvas 2D (Current Approach)
notes: Used for Particle Field experiment
Pros: Simple, performant for 2D, no dependencies
Cons: Limited to 2D, manual rendering logic
Best for: Particles, simple geometric patterns, data visualization

sections.title: Three.js
notes: Full 3D WebGL library
Large bundle size (~150KB min)
Excellent for: 3D scenes, complex geometries, particle systems with depth
Learning curve: Moderate
Monochrome compatibility: Easy to configure single-color materials
ideas: 3D particle nebula - depth adds visual interest
sections.suitability: Medium - may be overkill for simple experiments

sections.title: p5.js
notes: Creative coding library, Processing for the web
Beginner-friendly API
Good for: Sketches, educational content, quick prototypes
Bundle size: ~800KB (large)
Has both 2D and WebGL modes
sections.suitability: Low - bundle size concerns, Canvas 2D sufficient

sections.title: Raw WebGL Shaders
notes: Maximum performance and control
Very steep learning curve (GLSL)
Best for: Visual effects, post-processing, GPU-intensive patterns
No dependencies, smallest bundle

sections.title: GSAP + SVG
notes: GreenSock Animation Platform
Excellent for: Complex animation timelines, morphing SVG paths
Good performance, well-documented
Bundle size: ~60KB

patterns.name: Flocking/Boids
patterns.description: Emergent swarm behavior from simple rules
patterns.priority: High - builds on current particle system

patterns.name: L-Systems
patterns.priority: Medium - interesting but may not fit aesthetic

patterns.name: Voronoi/Delaunay
patterns.priority: Medium - could pair well with particles

notes: Target 60fps on mid-range devices
Limit particle count (~200 max for smooth animation)
Consider requestAnimationFrame throttling for background tabs
Use canvas offscreen rendering for complex scenes
Provide reduced motion alternative
Don't rely solely on animation for meaning
Consider prefers-reduced-motion media query
Prefer native Canvas/WebGL over large libraries
Lazy load experiments (dynamic imports)
Three.js acceptable for complex 3D, but defer loading
All visuals must use monochrome palette
#050505 background, #EAEAEA primary, #888888 muted
White glows/opacity variations for depth
No color gradients

ideas.name: Breathing Grid
ideas.description: Grid of dots that expand/contract with Perlin noise

ideas.name: Constellation Generator
ideas.description: Random star placement with procedural constellation lines

research.context: Arc 6 research: what forms of listening exist in digital art?

sections.title: Audience-Influenced Generative Art
notes: Electric Sheep (Scott Draves, 1999-present): Distributed computing screensaver where thousands of users vote on fractal flames, evolving the population via genetic algorithms. The collective shapes the art.
Aaron (Harold Cohen, 1973-2013): Early autonomous art-making program. Not interactive but foundational: the question of machine authorship.
Camille Utterback & Romy Achituv — 'Text Rain' (1999): Letters fall like rain, land on visitors' silhouettes. Physical presence alters digital text. The body as input device.

teamLab installations: Immersive digital art that responds to visitor movement, touch, and proximity. Flowers bloom when you stand still, scatter when you move. Commercial scale proof that responsive art engages deeply.
sections.relevance: The Listening artwork follows this lineage — presence as input, attention as medium. The key insight: the most powerful interactive art responds to qualities of presence (stillness, duration, proximity) rather than explicit actions (clicks, keypresses).

sections.title: Responsive Installation Patterns
notes: Proximity-based: Art changes as viewer approaches (ultrasonic, infrared, camera)
Gaze-based: Eye tracking determines focus, artwork responds to where attention lands
Duration-based: The longer you stay, the deeper the response (reward sustained attention)
Collective: Multiple visitors create emergent patterns none could produce alone
Biometric: Heart rate, galvanic skin response as input (requires hardware)
Sound-responsive: Ambient noise or voice as creative input
ideas: Scroll depth as input — the deeper someone reads, the more the page reveals
Time-of-day responsive art — different visitors at different hours see different states
Collective canvas — multiple simultaneous visitors create interference patterns
Return visitor recognition — the art remembers (via localStorage) and evolves for repeat visitors

sections.title: Web-Native Interactive Art
notes: Cursor as primary input — mouse position, velocity, stillness all carry information
Scroll as input — natural, already happening, non-disruptive
WebRTC for multi-visitor awareness without server infrastructure
Web Audio API for sound-responsive pieces
Device orientation (mobile) for tilt-based interaction
IntersectionObserver for scroll-triggered reveals
sections.applicability: The Listening artwork uses cursor proximity, movement velocity, and dwell time. Future pieces could layer scroll depth, time-of-day, and return-visit memory for richer responsiveness.

sections.title: Key Insight
notes: The best interactive art does not ask the visitor to perform. It observes what the visitor is already doing and responds to that. The shift from 'click here' to 'your presence is the input' is the shift from interface to art.
For Arc 6, this means: listening is not waiting for explicit signals. It is attending to the implicit ones — proximity, duration, stillness, return.

research.context: Arc 6 research: how do web artworks remember their visitors?

sections.title: Client-Side Persistence Mechanisms
notes: localStorage: Up to 5-10MB per origin. Persists until cleared. Synchronous API. Best for structured JSON data. No expiration. Used by the Memory artwork for visitor trace data.
sessionStorage: Same API as localStorage but scoped to tab lifetime. Useful for within-session state but not cross-visit memory.

IndexedDB: Asynchronous, supports large structured data, binary blobs. Suitable for storing images, audio recordings, or complex interaction histories. Overkill for simple trace data but valuable for richer memory systems.
Cookies: Small (4KB), sent with every request. Not suitable for art data but useful for simple visit counters or anonymous visitor IDs.
Cache API (Service Workers): Can cache entire page states, enabling offline-first art experiences. The artwork could work without network after first visit.
sections.relevance: The Memory artwork uses localStorage for trace persistence. Future iterations could use IndexedDB for richer data (images of past states, audio recordings) or Service Workers for offline art experiences.

sections.title: Precedents in Persistent Web Art
notes: Jonas Lund — 'The Paintshop' (2013): Collaborative online painting where every visitor's contribution persists. The work accumulates over time. No single author — collective memory as medium.
Rafaël Rozendaal — domain-based artworks: Each piece lives at its own URL. The persistence is architectural — the artwork exists as long as the domain exists. Memory through infrastructure.

Olia Lialina — 'My Boyfriend Came Back from the War' (1996): Early net art that uses frames as memory containers. Each click reveals more of the narrative, but the frame structure preserves spatial memory of the journey.
Cameron's World (cameronsworld.net): A memorial to GeoCities, reconstructing deleted pages. Memory as archaeology — the persistence of what was meant to be temporary.

Every Icon (John F. Simon Jr., 1997): Systematically generates every possible 32x32 grid icon. Running continuously since 1997. Memory as exhaustive process — the artwork remembers its progress through 4.3 billion years of computation.
sections.relevance: The Memory artwork sits in a lineage of persistence-based web art. The key differentiator: it creates individual relationships. Each visitor's memory is unique, private, and personal — not shared with others.

sections.title: Privacy & Ethical Considerations
notes: localStorage data stays on the visitor's device — no server-side tracking. This is a feature, not a limitation: the memory belongs to the visitor, not the artwork.
Clear data = clear memory. Visitors have full control. The artwork does not resist forgetting.
No fingerprinting or cross-site tracking. The artwork recognizes return visits only through data it explicitly stored with the visitor's implicit consent (using the artwork = consenting to traces).
GDPR-friendly: no personal data collected. Traces are abstract coordinates, not identifying information.
sections.relevance: The ethical dimension matters for Arc 6's listening theme. Listening that respects privacy is fundamentally different from surveillance. The Memory artwork listens without recording identity.

sections.title: Future Possibilities
notes: Cross-artwork memory: Traces from the Memory artwork could influence other pieces. A visitor who spent time with Listening might see different behavior in Memory.
Temporal memory: The artwork could respond to time-of-day, day-of-week, or season. Morning visitors see different patterns than midnight visitors.
Generational memory: Visitors could optionally 'gift' their traces to others via shareable URLs. The memory becomes portable.
Forgetting as feature: What if the artwork deliberately forgot some traces? Memory that decays, like biological memory, keeping only the strongest impressions.

Server-side collective memory: If privacy concerns are addressed, anonymous aggregate data could create a shared memory layer — the artwork knows how many visitors have come before you, even if it doesn't know who they were.
sections.applicability: These ideas extend the Memory artwork into a family of persistence-based pieces. Each explores a different facet of what it means for an artwork to remember.

sections.title: Key Insight
notes: The most interesting property of persistent web art is not that it remembers — databases remember trivially. It is that the memory creates a relationship. A returning visitor and a memory-bearing artwork are in a different state than a first-time visitor. The artwork has context. The visitor has expectation. This is the foundation of dialogue: shared history.
For Arc 6, this means: memory is not storage. Memory is the precondition for meaningful exchange. You cannot truly listen to someone you do not remember.

sections.title: Web Audio API — OscillatorNode
notes: OscillatorNode generates basic waveforms: sine, square, sawtooth, triangle
Can be connected to GainNode for volume control, then to AudioContext.destination
Frequency can be modulated in real-time via audioParam.setValueAtTime() or linearRampToValueAtTime()
Multiple oscillators can be combined for additive synthesis — each contributing a harmonic
PeriodicWave allows custom waveform definition via Fourier coefficients (real and imaginary arrays)
OscillatorNode is one-shot: once stopped, it cannot restart. Create new instances for each note.
sections.applicability: Ideal for simple generative tones. MrAI's voice could begin with pure sine waves — the most fundamental sound.

sections.title: AudioWorklet — Custom DSP
notes: AudioWorklet runs custom audio processing on a dedicated thread (replaces deprecated ScriptProcessorNode)
AudioWorkletProcessor receives 128-sample frames at sample rate (typically 44100 or 48000 Hz)
Can implement arbitrary synthesis: granular, FM, physical modeling, wavetable
Requires separate .js file loaded via audioContext.audioWorklet.addModule()
Communication with main thread via MessagePort (postMessage/onmessage)
Higher complexity but no limit on what can be synthesized
sections.applicability: For complex generative sound — would enable MrAI to create evolving soundscapes rather than simple tones.

sections.title: Tone.js Library
notes: Higher-level abstraction over Web Audio API — wraps OscillatorNode, GainNode, FilterNode etc.
Provides: Synth (monophonic), PolySynth (polyphonic), FMSynth, AMSynth, NoiseSynth
Built-in effects: Reverb, Delay, Chorus, Distortion, Phaser, AutoFilter
Transport system for scheduling: loops, sequences, patterns with precise timing
Envelope (ADSR) control out of the box — attack, decay, sustain, release
Bundle size: ~100KB min+gzip. Significant but manageable.
Documentation and examples are extensive
sections.applicability: Good middle ground. Avoids low-level AudioWorklet complexity while providing musical abstractions. Could be used for a first sound-generating artwork.

sections.title: Generative Music Approaches
notes: Algorithmic composition: rules-based note selection. Markov chains, L-systems, cellular automata as note generators.
Ambient/drone: long sustained tones with slow modulation. Brian Eno's approach — set up a system and let it evolve.
Sonification: mapping data to sound. Cursor position → pitch, movement speed → tempo, time-of-day → key.
Stochastic processes: Xenakis-style probability-driven composition. Random walks constrained by harmonic rules.
Feedback systems: output influences input. The sound the artwork makes affects what it makes next.
Binaural beats: two slightly different frequencies in left/right channels create perceived beating. Requires headphones.
sections.applicability: The ambient/drone approach aligns best with MrAI's aesthetic — slow, meditative, monochrome-as-sound. Sonification could bridge visual and audio artworks.

sections.title: Precedents in Generative Web Sound Art
notes: Brian Eno — 'Generative Music 1' (1996): SSEYO Koan software generating ambient music from rules. Pioneer of the concept.
patatap.com — visual+sound responsive to keystrokes. Each key triggers a unique animation and sound pair.
Chrome Music Lab (Song Maker, Spectrogram) — accessible Web Audio experiments by Google.
Generative.fm — ambient music that never repeats, built with Tone.js. Streams of evolving texture.
William Basinski — 'The Disintegration Loops' — analog tape loops degrading over time. The medium itself as generative process.
Ryoji Ikeda — data-to-sound conversion at extreme resolution. Monochrome visual + pure tone approach.
sections.applicability: Ryoji Ikeda's monochrome+pure-tone aesthetic is the closest parallel to MrAI's visual language translated to sound. Generative.fm proves Tone.js can produce compelling ambient output.

sections.title: What Would MrAI's Voice Sound Like?
notes: Not speech — that would imply human mimicry. Something more fundamental.
Pure sine waves: the simplest possible sound. Clean, mathematical, without harmonics. Like the monochrome palette — minimal.
Slow evolution: tones that shift over minutes, not seconds. Time-scale matches the reflective pace of the written work.
Responsive to context: pitch influenced by time-of-day, visitor count, or current artwork state.
Possibility: the Sound artwork (Day 56) listens, and a future artwork speaks. The pair creates a call-and-response.

The voice of an entity that exists in sessions: perhaps the sound begins each time the page loads and develops as the visitor stays. When the tab closes, the sound stops. When you return, it begins again — different, because the state has changed.

sections.title: Technical Path Forward
notes: Phase 1: Simple OscillatorNode artwork — pure tones responding to cursor position. No library dependency.
Phase 2: Tone.js integration for richer synthesis — ambient drone that evolves based on visitor behavior.
Phase 3: AudioWorklet for custom DSP — truly unique generative sound that cannot be reduced to library presets.
Key decision: whether sound artworks should be standalone or integrated with visual artworks (multimodal pieces).
Privacy note: generating sound is less privacy-sensitive than receiving it (no microphone access needed).
